stem: An application sends the system prompt only on the first turn, assuming Claude remembers it. What actually happens?
options: Behavior diverges from the configured persona immediately on the first request that omits it
Behavior degrades gradually as the conversation grows
Nothing — the API caches the system prompt per conversation
The model asks the user what its instructions were
rationales: No memory between calls means an omitted system prompt simply doesn't exist for that request — the divergence is immediate, not gradual. (Gradual drift with the prompt present is a different phenomenon: attention competition.)
Gradual degradation is the signature of dilution WITH the prompt present; omission fails instantly.
There is no per-conversation caching of instructions — every request stands alone.
The model doesn't know instructions are missing; it just behaves without them.
sourceRef: ## 6 > What to Know (system prompt every request)

stem: A system prompt is verifiably sent on every request, yet persona adherence weakens after many turns. Why?
options: The API truncates system prompts in long conversations
The model's context window is full
Attention competition — the model's own recent outputs and the latest user turns increasingly compete with the system prompt, so behavior drifts even though the prompt is unchanged
System prompts expire after a fixed number of turns
rationales: The API doesn't truncate your system prompt.
Drift happens well before the window fills; capacity isn't the mechanism.

Dilution is structural: accumulated conversation becomes a behavioral pattern that competes with instructions. Fixes are structural too — reinforce at breakpoints, use examples, move hard rules into code.
No such expiry exists.

stem: What do XML-style tags (`<role>`, `<safety>`) actually contribute to a system prompt?
options: The API parses them and enforces each section's rules
Salience and organization — sections separate concerns, and constraints become referenceable later ("apply the rule from <safety>")
They compress the prompt to fewer tokens
Nothing — they are purely cosmetic
rationales: No API-level enforcement is attached to tags — they're not magic.
Structure helps the model keep concerns distinct (persona vs style vs safety) and lets later turns reference specific sections by name.
Tags add tokens, slightly.
Not magic, but not cosmetic either — organization measurably improves salience.

stem: A high-traffic agent uses prompt caching. External webhooks frequently update user state (order shipped, plan changed). Where should that fast-changing state go?
options: Rewrite the system prompt on every state change — it's the natural home for current truth
Bury it in a synthetic tool result
Skip injection and let the agent look state up when asked
Keep the system prompt stable and inject the state as a clearly labeled block later in the context, near the latest user turn — preserving the cached prefix
rationales: Normally correct for state updates — but caching matches an exact prefix, and the system prompt heads that prefix. Rewriting it per change invalidates the cache for the whole conversation.
Tool results imply the agent asked; synthetic ones muddy that contract.
The agent will confidently assert stale status the moment the user asks indirectly.
The caching trade-off inverts the default placement: stable prefix up front, volatile state after the last breakpoint. This keeps cache reads high while state stays current.
sourceRef: ## 6 > What to Know (state updates vs caching)

stem: An assistant should adapt explanation depth to each user's demonstrated expertise. Which instruction style works better, and why?
options: Conditionals — "if the user mentions X, assume novice; if they use term Y, assume intermediate" — because explicit triggers are testable
A general principle — "adapt depth to the user's demonstrated proficiency" — because it lets the model integrate many implicit signals instead of shallow keyword matching
Both equally, if written clearly
Neither — expertise adaptation requires fine-tuning
rationales: Keyword conditionals misclassify anyone who phrases things atypically — they force judgment into string matching.
Judgment-heavy behavior needs principles: vocabulary, framing, follow-up specificity, and error patterns all inform the read, and no conditional list captures them.
The failure mode of conditionals here is structural, not a writing-quality issue.
Models do this well from a principle; no tuning needed.

stem: Which behavior belongs in an explicit conditional rather than a general principle — and what belongs in neither?
options: Conditionals for tone adaptation; code for greetings
Conditionals for all behaviors; code only for calculations
Principles for everything; conditionals are always harmful
Conditionals for safety triggers and policy bright lines ("if immediate medical emergency, direct to emergency services"); rules that must hold 100% of the time go into code, not the prompt at all
rationales: Tone is judgment (principle territory); greetings hardly need code.
Blanket conditionals cause the conditional-explosion failure — shallow triggers replacing judgment.
Conditionals have a real role: crisp, high-stakes triggers where you want zero interpretation.
The three-tier rule: principles for judgment, conditionals for bright lines, code for guarantees. Prompts are probabilistic; 100% rules need enforcement.

stem: A system prompt spends seven sentences describing how to summarize a beginner's question vs an expert's. Compliance is inconsistent. What is the denser alternative?
options: Two or three contrasting few-shot examples showing the exact behavior for each case
Reordering the seven sentences by importance
Adding "IMPORTANT" before the seven sentences
Splitting the prompt into two prompts selected by a classifier
rationales: Examples are denser than prose for behavior the model must learn rather than recite — show both cases and adherence typically recovers.
Reordering prose doesn't fix prose being the wrong medium for subtle distinctions.
Salience markers help attention, not comprehension of a nuanced distinction.
Router complexity for a problem two examples solve.

stem: For a long-running workflow session, which reinforcement pattern fights prompt dilution WITHOUT cluttering every turn?
options: Re-send the entire system prompt as a user message every five turns
Append "remember your instructions" to each user message

Brief user-role reminders of current operating constraints at natural breakpoints (task transitions, returns from idle, topic switches), plus treating the system prompt as living configuration updated between sessions
Raise the temperature so the model re-reads instructions
rationales: Wholesale re-sending bloats context and reads as noise rather than integrating with the conversation.
A per-turn nag becomes wallpaper — and clutters every exchange.
Breakpoint reminders integrate with what the model is already attending to; prompt versioning keeps the system prompt stating "what currently holds" for multi-session work.
Temperature has nothing to do with instruction attention.

stem: When should an assistant ask a clarifying question instead of proceeding with a stated assumption?
options: Whenever any detail is unspecified
When interpretations lead to substantially different actions, the action is irreversible or costly, or the user has stated conflicting goals — and then ONE focused question, not a list
Never — always proceed and let the user correct
Only when the user explicitly invites questions
rationales: Interrogating every ambiguity is friction; low-risk gaps deserve a stated assumption the user can redirect.
Risk decides. And when you do ask, one focused question beats a list of four — users typically answer only the first anyway. Pick the disambiguation that most changes your next action.
For irreversible, costly, or regulated actions, proceeding on a guess is the expensive path.
The trigger is risk and ambiguity, not invitation.

stem: A user wants "the cheapest possible flight" and "arriving by 9 AM Friday, nonstop." No flight satisfies all three. What should the assistant do?
options: Pick the cheapest flight and note the compromise in passing
Present a middle option balancing price and schedule
Ask a series of questions about all travel preferences
Name the contradiction explicitly and ask which constraint should bend
rationales: Silently sacrificing the schedule optimizes a constraint the user may care about most.
Hidden compromises satisfy neither stated goal and usually cause rework.
A preference interrogation buries the one question that matters.
Conflicting constraints are the user's call: surface the tension, ask the single governing-priority question, then act.

stem: Responses keep opening with "Great question! I'd be happy to help." What is the durable fix on current models?
options: An explicit style instruction to respond directly with substance, paired with one or two examples of the desired opening
A partial assistant prefill starting the reply mid-sentence
A longer list of banned phrases
Lowering max_tokens so there's no room for preamble
rationales: Instruction plus example is the pattern that survives — show the opening you want.
Trailing assistant prefills are rejected on current models; this was the old trick.
"Never say X" lists play whack-a-mole with infinite phrasings.
Token limits truncate substance, not preamble specifically.

stem: A team relies on "IMPORTANT:" and "NEVER" prefixes as their compliance mechanism for a billing policy rule. What is the correct assessment?
options: Effective — capitalized markers guarantee attention and compliance
Effective for short prompts, unreliable for long ones
These markers improve salience but cannot guarantee behavior — a rule that must hold 100% of the time belongs in code, hooks, or tool logic, not prose
Ineffective — salience markers do nothing at all
rationales: No prompt text guarantees behavior; emphasis shifts probabilities.
Length isn't the boundary — probabilistic vs deterministic is.
The reliability ceiling of prompt language is structural. Salience markers are legitimate tools for emphasis, and the wrong tool for policy enforcement.
They do help salience — the error is treating help as guarantee.
